GIT

NAME
SYNOPSIS
DESCRIPTION
OPTIONS
GIT COMMANDS
SEE ALSO

NAME

git − the stupid content tracker

SYNOPSIS

git [−v | −−version] [−h | −−help] [−C <path>] [−c <name>=<value>]
[−−exec−path[=<path>]] [−−html−path] [−−man−path] [−−info−path]
[−p | −−paginate | −P | −−no−pager] [−−no−replace−objects] [−−no−lazy−fetch]
<command> [<args>]

DESCRIPTION

Git is a fast, scalable, distributed revision control system with an unusually rich command set that provides both high−level operations and full access to internals.

See gittutorial(7) to get started, then see giteveryday(7) for a useful minimum set of commands. The Git User’s Manual [1] has a more in−depth introduction.

After you mastered the basic concepts, you can come back to this page to learn what commands Git offers.

OPTIONS

−v, −−version

Prints the synopsis and a list of the most commonly used commands. If the option −−all or −a is given then all available commands are printed.

Other options are available to control how the manual page is displayed.

−C <path>

Run as if git was started in <path> instead of the current working directory.

For example the following invocations are equivalent:

git −−git−dir=a.git −−work−tree=b −C c status
git −−git−dir=c/a.git −−work−tree=c/b status

−c <name>=<value>

Pass a configuration parameter to the command. The value given will override values from configuration files.

GIT COMMANDS

We divide Git into high level (porcelain) commands and low level (plumbing) commands.

High−Level Commands (Porcelain)

We separate the porcelain commands into the main commands and the ancillary commands.

SEE ALSO

gittutorial(7), git−cli(7), gitworkflows(7).
